title: Logarithms in Regression

q: What does a binary predictor in a regression actually do?
a:
It compares means — the coefficient is the difference in the outcome's mean between the yes group
and the no group.
title: Categorical Predictors

q: Which classical test is that equivalent to?
a: A two-sample t-test.
title: Categorical Predictors

q: So why use a regression instead?
a:
Because you can add control variables and interpret the coefficient as the effect adjusting for
them — plus you get a standard error and significance test.
title: Categorical Predictors

q: How does the mantra explain the binary case?
a:
A one-unit change in X is associated with a coefficient-sized change in Y, and for a binary
variable the only sensible one-unit change is going from 0 to 1.
title: Categorical Predictors

q: Sales are 10 in winter and 15 otherwise. Give the intercept and the winter coefficient.
a: Intercept 15 (the not-winter average) and −5 on winter.
title: Categorical Predictors

q: Why must the intercept equal the not-winter average?
a:
Setting winter to zero leaves only the intercept, so it has to deliver the best prediction for
not-winter, which is that group's mean.
title: Categorical Predictors

q: How do you include a categorical variable with seven categories?
a: Create a binary indicator for each category, then include all but one in the regression.
title: Categorical Predictors

q: What is the omitted category called, and what happens to the observations in it?
a: The reference group. Those observations remain in the data — they become the intercept.
title: Categorical Predictors

q: Why does Nick prefer "binary indicator" to "dummy"?
a:
Because saying something like "I put the female dummy in the model" to someone who doesn't know
econometrics may not go over well.
title: Categorical Predictors

q: With Asia omitted, what does the coefficient on Europe mean?
a: The difference in means between Europe and Asia — not the effect of being in Europe.
title: Categorical Predictors

q: France 30, Gambia 30.5, New Zealand 33, with France as reference. Give all three terms.
a: Intercept 30, Gambia 0.5, New Zealand 3.
title: Categorical Predictors

q: Now switch the reference group to Gambia. What do France and New Zealand become?
a: France −0.5 and New Zealand 2.5.
title: Categorical Predictors

q: What's the implication of that for significance tests?
a:
They're arbitrary — they test against whichever reference group you picked. A coefficient's
significance answers "is this different from the reference?", which may not be your question.
title: Categorical Predictors

q: How do you test whether a categorical variable matters overall?
a:
A joint F test on all its indicators, with the null that every one of those coefficients is zero
— i.e. every category is identical to the reference group.
title: Categorical Predictors

q: What does an interaction term let you do that a polynomial doesn't?
a:
Let the effect of X vary across values of a *different* variable Z, rather than across values
of X itself.
title: Interaction Terms

q: What's the most common application in causal inference?
a:
Estimating how a treatment effect differs across groups — a conditional average treatment
effect.
title: Interaction Terms

q: Does adding Z as a control let the effect of X differ across values of Z?
a:
No. It moves the line up and down but leaves the slope on X unchanged. You need the interaction
term.
title: Interaction Terms

q: Why must you include Z on its own when including X×Z?
a:
Otherwise Z goes into the error term, and since X×Z is related to Z, the interaction
coefficient combines the interaction effect with Z's effect — omitted variable bias.
title: Interaction Terms

q: Write the derivative of Y with respect to X for the model y = β₀ + β₁x + β₂z + β₃(x·z).
a: dy/dx = β₁ + β₃z.
title: Interaction Terms

q: What is β₁ in that model?
a:
The effect of x on y among the group where z = 0 — plugging in z = 0 makes the β₃z term drop
out.
title: Interaction Terms

q: What is the effect of x when z = 1?
a: β₁ + β₃.
title: Interaction Terms

q: What does β₃ mean?
a:
The difference in the effect of x on y between the z = 1 group and the z = 0 group — how much
the effect changes going from one group to the other.
title: Interaction Terms

q: How does β₃ relate to the binary-indicator interpretation from Ep 31?
a:
It's the same "difference between groups" idea, except the left-hand side is the derivative of
y with respect to x rather than y itself — so it's a difference in effects, not a difference in
means.
title: Interaction Terms

q: Locations coefficient −0.019, interaction with weekend −0.010. Give the effect on weekdays and on weekends.
title: Interaction Terms

q: That interaction wasn't statistically significant. What can you conclude?
a:
Not that the effects are the same — only that you lack sufficient evidence to reject that
they're the same on weekends and weekdays.
title: Interaction Terms

q: Why are interaction terms especially noisy?
a:
With a binary interaction you're effectively estimating each effect using half the sample size,
and halving the sample makes estimates noisy quickly.
title: Interaction Terms

q: What's the practical rule before adding an interaction?
a:
Have a reason for it. Don't add interactions to try them out and see what turns up — enough
combinations will always produce something significant.
title: Interaction Terms

q: Why can the standard error formula be wrong?
a:
It isn't the definition of the sampling distribution's standard deviation — it's a calculation
resting on assumptions about the error term. If those fail, it stops reflecting the sampling
distribution.
title: Your Standard Errors are Wrong

q: When these assumptions fail, what breaks and what survives?
a: The estimate of the standard error breaks. The OLS coefficient is usually fine.
title: Your Standard Errors are Wrong

q: What exactly does the normality assumption apply to?
a: The error term ε — not your X and Y variables, which is the common misunderstanding.
title: Your Standard Errors are Wrong

q: What actually goes wrong if errors aren't normally distributed?
a:
You can no longer prove the OLS coefficient is normally distributed. It may follow another
distribution, but usually one close to normal unless the errors are very strange.
title: Your Standard Errors are Wrong

q: What does i.i.d. stand for, and what does each part mean?
a:
Independent and identically distributed. Identically distributed: every error follows the same
distribution. Independent: they're uncorrelated, so knowing one tells you nothing about another.
title: Your Standard Errors are Wrong

q: Define heteroskedasticity.
a: When the variance of the error term is related to your predictors X.
title: Your Standard Errors are Wrong

q: You assumed X is unrelated to the error term. Why doesn't that rule out heteroskedasticity?
a:
Because X being unrelated to the error term itself doesn't prevent it from being related to the
error term's *variance*.
title: Your Standard Errors are Wrong

q: Describe the fan shape.
a:
At low values of X the residuals have little spread; as X increases the spread grows. It's one
example of heteroskedasticity, not the only shape.
title: Your Standard Errors are Wrong

q: What is autocorrelation and where does it arise?
a:
Errors correlated with each other across time — arising in time series data or panel data, and
essentially any time you have a repeatedly measured value.
title: Your Standard Errors are Wrong

q: Use the unemployment example to explain why errors are autocorrelated.
a:
If you're above the fitted line this period, you're likely above it next period too. Being above
the line is the residual, which represents the error term — so errors correlate across time.
title: Your Standard Errors are Wrong

q: What is clustering, and give the example.
a:
Hierarchically collected data producing grouped errors. Students at the same school have more
similar scores than students at different schools, and within a school, students sharing a
classroom and teacher are more similar still — so their residuals and errors cluster.
title: Your Standard Errors are Wrong

q: Why do these failures break the standard error calculation?
a:
σ² is supposed to measure the variance of the error terms, and computing it while ignoring
heteroskedasticity, autocorrelation, or clustering measures it badly — leaving you ignorant of how
sampling variation actually behaves. The variance of X term can also misbehave.
title: Your Standard Errors are Wrong

q: What is a sandwich estimator doing?
a:
The same standard error calculation, but computing σ² and the variance of X in ways that account
for the specific problem.
title: Your Standard Errors are Wrong

q: Name the three fixes.
a:
Heteroskedasticity-robust standard errors; heteroskedasticity- and autocorrelation-consistent
(HAC) standard errors; and cluster-robust standard errors.
title: Your Standard Errors are Wrong

q: How does the heteroskedasticity-robust version handle the fan graph?
a:
It inflates the influence of the high-X observations, where variance is large, and shrinks the
influence of the low-X ones — giving an error-variance estimate closer to the sampling variation
you'd really get.
title: Your Standard Errors are Wrong

q: What do many economists do by default?
a:
Always use heteroskedasticity-robust standard errors, assuming it's always a problem — which
Nick considers reasonable.
title: Your Standard Errors are Wrong

q: What does basic OLS quietly assume about the dependent variable?
a: That it's continuous — able to take any value in a range without real restriction.
title: Probit and Logit

q: Name some dependent variables that violate that.
a:
Counts (non-negative integers), continuous variables that can't be negative or can't be
positive, and binary outcomes.
title: Probit and Logit

q: What is a linear probability model?
a:
Ordinary least squares run with a binary dependent variable — treating the probability of the
outcome linearly.
title: Probit and Logit

q: Give the first problem with a linear probability model.
a: The fitted line extends beyond 0 and 1, so it predicts impossible probabilities like 150% or −2.
title: Probit and Logit

q: Give the second problem, with the college example.
a:
A straight line has a constant slope. Going from a 50% chance of college, tutoring might add
five percentage points — but someone already at 99.999% has no room, so the same tutoring might
move them only from 99.9% to 99.92%. The slope should flatten near the edges.
title: Probit and Logit

q: What is the index function?
a: β₀ + β₁x — the predictors multiplied by coefficients and added linearly, exactly what OLS does.
title: Probit and Logit

q: What is the link function for, and what's the only difference between OLS and a generalized linear model?
a:
The link function maps the index — which can be any value — into the scale of the dependent
variable, here 0 to 1. Passing the index through it is the only difference from OLS.
title: Probit and Logit

q: Which link functions do logit and probit use?
a:
Logit uses the logistic function; probit uses the cumulative distribution function of the
standard normal distribution.
title: Probit and Logit

q: Which two problems does the link function solve?
a:
Predictions can no longer fall outside 0 to 1, and the curve flattens near the edges while
staying steep in the middle.
title: Probit and Logit

q: When is it most important to use probit or logit rather than a linear probability model?
a:
When you think the probability of the outcome is close to 100% or close to 0%, rather than in
the middle.
title: Probit and Logit

q: Why do many people use the linear probability model anyway?
a:
Probit and logit introduce computational difficulties, including trouble with fixed effects. If
all you want is the slope, the cost may not be worth it.
title: Probit and Logit

q: Why can't you interpret a logit coefficient directly?
a:
It describes how x relates to the *index function*, which must still pass through a
transformation to become the probability that y = 1. The index is hard to interpret.
title: Probit and Logit

q: What's a marginal effect?
a:
The change in the predicted *probability* from changing x by a unit — putting the effect back on
the scale a linear probability model would have given you.
title: Probit and Logit

q: Why isn't there a single marginal effect?
a:
The marginal effect is the slope of the curve, and the slope differs at every point on the X
axis, so each individual has their own.
title: Probit and Logit

q: What is the average marginal effect, and why does Nick recommend it?
a:
Compute each data point's own marginal effect — the slope of the curve at that point — then
average them. He recommends it because averaging each person's own marginal effect is typically
what people actually want.
title: Probit and Logit

q: What are you actually doing when you close a back door?
a: Removing all the variation through the variable on that back door.
title: Matching

q: How does regression remove that variation?
a: By subtracting out whatever it can predict with the confounder and using just the residual.
title: Matching

q: How does matching remove it instead?
a:
By picking a sample in which that variable doesn't vary — or in which the treated and control
groups have no differences in it.
title: Matching

q: Give the temperature intuition.
a:
Look only at hot days (or only cold days). Within that sample temperature isn't varying, so it
can't be driving the relationship between shorts and ice cream.
title: Matching

q: Treated group is 80 men and 20 women; control is 500 men and 500 women. Why can't you compare directly?
a:
A difference in job-getting rates could be the program or could be the gender imbalance — you
couldn't tell which. That's an open back door.
title: Matching

q: What's the matched-sample fix?
a: Select 80 men and 20 women from the control group, so both groups have the same gender mix.
title: Matching

q: Name matching's two advantages over regression.
a:
It doesn't rely as heavily on linearity assumptions, and it offers more flexibility in
estimating different kinds of treatment effect averages.
title: Matching

q: Name matching's two disadvantages.
a:
It requires far more decision making, and results can be much more sensitive to those
decisions.
title: Matching

q: What are the two main approaches to matching?
a:
Selecting a matched sample (a subset of controls resembling the treated), or weighting the
controls so their weighted average resembles the treated group.
title: Matching

q: Two people earn $100,000 and $200,000. What's the weighted average if the first gets double weight?
a:
(100,000 + 100,000 + 200,000) / 3 ≈ $133,000 — closer to the first person because they count
more.
title: Matching

q: Treated group has 80 men and 20 women; control has 500 of each. Give the weights.
a: Each control man gets 80/500 = 0.16; each control woman gets 20/500 = 0.04.
title: Matching

q: State the bias–variance trade-off in matching.
a:
Fewer, better-matched observations mean less bias but more noise. More observations, including
worse matches, mean more precision but more bias.
title: Matching

q: Which side of that trade-off does weighting sit on, and why?
a:
Lower variance, higher bias — you get to use everybody, which is more precise, but some of those
are worse comparisons.
title: Matching

q: What is the goal that all five questions serve?
a:
Getting a treated group and control group that are comparable across the variables you match on
— each matched variable closes a back door.
title: Five Questions About Matching

q: Describe the running example's treatment, outcome, and matching variable.
a:
Treatment: late with your April credit card bill. Outcome: late in September. Matching variable:
the size of your April bill.
title: Five Questions About Matching

q: Why is April bill size a back door here?
a:
Bigger April bills mean bigger September bills, so large bills make you more likely to be late in
both months — which would look like stickiness when it's really difficulty paying.
title: Five Questions About Matching

q: Why can't your matching criterion be exact equality?
a:
With a continuous variable essentially nobody shares an exact value — if a bill is 47,432, no
control observation matches it — so you'd find no matches at all.
title: Five Questions About Matching

q: With a single matching variable, what is the criterion?
a:
Simply the difference between the values. 43,000 versus 43,001 is a difference of 1, so very
close; larger differences mean more different.
title: Five Questions About Matching

q: Why does the criterion get harder with more variables?
a:
Two candidates may be similar on one variable and less similar on another, and you need a
measure of distance that combines them.
title: Five Questions About Matching

q: Describe one-to-one matching.
a:
For each treated observation, pick the single closest control observation as its comparison and
discard everything else.
title: Five Questions About Matching

q: Describe the weighting alternative.
a:
Keep many control observations, giving the closest ones the largest weights and shrinking the
weights as distance grows, until beyond some distance they count for nothing.
title: Five Questions About Matching

q: Give the bias–variance trade-off between those two.
a:
Picking only the best matches gives lowest bias but a very small, noisy control group. Weighting
admits worse comparisons — more bias — but far more observations, so more precision.
title: Five Questions About Matching

q: If you're selecting matches, what happens as you take more of them?
a:
Each successive match is worse than the last, adding bias, but the comparison group grows, adding
precision.
title: Five Questions About Matching

q: What are the two sub-decisions in how weights decay?
a: How fast they decay, and what function — what kernel — describes the decay.
title: Five Questions About Matching

q: What is a kernel?
a:
A function maximised at the centre, where you're closest to the treated observation, and getting
smaller as you move away — determining the weights.
title: Five Questions About Matching

q: Name two kernels and how they differ.
a:
The triangular kernel decays in a straight line; the Epanechnikov kernel is more curved, decaying
more slowly at first and then more quickly.
title: Five Questions About Matching

q: A treated observation has a 1,000 bill and the closest control has 6,000. What are your options?
a:
Accept it as a match despite being incomparable, or declare it too bad and drop that treated
observation from the study entirely.
title: Five Questions About Matching

q: How does question 5 show up when using weights rather than matches?
a:
The kernel eventually goes to zero — beyond some distance an observation gets no weight at all —
and you decide how quickly it reaches zero.
title: Five Questions About Matching

q: State the bias–variance trade-off in accepting worse matches.
a:
Accepting worse matches keeps more treated observations in the sample, giving more precision, but
compares incomparable things, so the back door isn't really closed and bias returns.
title: Five Questions About Matching

q: Do these five questions apply only to certain matching methods?
a:
No — you generally face all of them for any matching procedure, though some methods let you skip
one or two.
title: Five Questions About Matching

q: What does distance matching ask?
a: How far apart two observations are on the matching variables — then pick the closest ones.
title: Distance Matching

q: Why is one matching variable easy?
a: The distance is simply the difference between the two values.
title: Distance Matching

q: I'm 35 with a 6,000 bill. Candidate A has a 6,000 bill and is 60; candidate B is 35 with a 1,000 bill. What's the problem?
a:
You have two separate distances — one on bill, one on age — and no way to say who's closer until
you combine them into a single distance.
title: Distance Matching

q: What metaphor is used to combine multiple distances?
a:
Physical distance in space — separate distances along each axis collapse into one straight line
between two points, and it generalises beyond three dimensions.
title: Distance Matching

q: What is Euclidean distance?
a:
The straight-line distance computed with the Pythagorean theorem from the distances along each
axis.
title: Distance Matching

q: What does Mahalanobis distance add, and why?
a:
It divides out the correlations between the matching variables, so a shared underlying component
isn't double-counted.
title: Distance Matching

q: Give the arm hair and beard example.
a:
If you want to match on gender but can't measure it, arm hair and having a beard both track being
a man. Using both counts "being a man" twice, so Mahalanobis removes the correlation and lets the
shared component count once.
title: Distance Matching

q: Does Mahalanobis drop one of the correlated variables?
a: No. Both remain as matching variables; only the double-counted overlap is subtracted out.
title: Distance Matching

q: Once you have a single distance measure, what can you do with it?
a:
Pick the single best match, pick the several best matches, or construct weights where the shortest
distances get the largest weights.
title: Distance Matching

q: Define the curse of dimensionality in this context.
a:
As you add matching variables — and therefore dimensions — observations tend to get further and
further apart, so decent matches become harder to find.
title: Distance Matching

q: Give the building illustration.
a:
Two people look identically placed when viewed from directly above — both in the northeast corner
— but adding the vertical dimension reveals one is on the fifth floor and the other on the first.
title: Distance Matching

q: What practical problem does this create for the "worst acceptable match" question?
a:
With many dimensions, every match looks bad — so you may reject nearly all of them unless you
loosen your standard.
title: Distance Matching

q: What's the usual mitigation, and what's its limitation?
a:
Being more lax about the worst acceptable match as you add matching variables. It's a mitigation
rather than a fix — the matches genuinely are worse.
title: Distance Matching

q: What's the insight behind propensity score matching?
a:
Closing back doors doesn't require removing all variation in the confounders — only the part of
them that determines whether someone is treated.
title: Propensity Score Matching

q: Define the propensity score.
a: The probability that somebody is going to be treated.
title: Propensity Score Matching

q: How is it usually estimated?
a:
With a probit or logit model: regress whether you were treated on the matching variables and
predict the probability.
title: Propensity Score Matching

q: What other approach is increasingly used, and why does it fit?
a:
Machine learning — because estimating a propensity score is a prediction problem, and prediction
is what machine learning is good at.
title: Propensity Score Matching

q: What does using a regression to estimate it cost you?
a: The linearity assumption you avoided by using matching instead of regression comes back in.
title: Propensity Score Matching

q: Why doesn't propensity score matching suffer the curse of dimensionality?
a:
Because it collapses all the matching variables into a single dimension — the score — rather than
measuring distance separately along each.
title: Propensity Score Matching

q: Should you use the propensity score to pick a set of matches?
a: No. That has been shown to have statistical issues and doesn't work well. Use weighting instead.
title: Propensity Score Matching

q: Give the inverse probability weights for treated and control observations.
a:
Treated: 1 divided by the probability of being treated. Control: 1 divided by the probability of
*not* being treated.
title: Propensity Score Matching

q: Which treated observations end up counting most, and why?
a:
Those least likely to have been treated. The control group didn't get treated, so treated
observations resembling the control group are the ones that were unlikely to be treated.
title: Propensity Score Matching

q: Which control observations count most?
a: Those most likely to have been treated but weren't — since they most resemble the treated group.
title: Propensity Score Matching

q: What goes wrong with extreme propensity scores, and how do you fix it?
a:
Weights become 1 divided by something near zero and blow up. Trim the score — drop observations
with very high or very low probabilities of treatment.
title: Propensity Score Matching

q: What is common support?
a:
Whether there actually exist untreated people with a high probability of treatment, and treated
people with a low probability. Without them you have no comparable observations.
title: Propensity Score Matching

q: How do you handle a lack of common support?
a:
Drop observations whose probabilities have nobody on the other side — for instance, if no control
had an 80%+ chance of treatment, drop treated observations above 80%.
title: Propensity Score Matching

q: What is a failure of balance?
a:
When groups are well matched on the propensity score but the individual matching variables still
look very different between them.
title: Propensity Score Matching

q: Give the balance example.
a:
Matching on April bill and age, both of which raise the probability of not paying: someone with a
very high bill who is very young and someone with a very low bill who is very old can have identical
propensity scores while being nothing alike.
title: Propensity Score Matching

q: Is poor balance technically a problem, and what do people do about it?
a:
Technically the score is what you're controlling for, so it needn't be — but it looks suspicious,
so people check whether the matching variables are at least roughly similar across groups.
title: Propensity Score Matching

q: Describe the check-and-return approach.
a:
Estimate the propensity score, do the matching, check common support and balance; if they look
bad, go back and change the estimation — add polynomial terms, add or remove matching variables — and
repeat.
title: Propensity Score Matching

q: What flaw do distance and propensity score matching share?
a:
They tend to fail — you can run them and still end up with matched groups that don't look alike,
forcing you to go back and change something and try again.
title: Coarsened Exact Matching and Entropy Balancing

q: What is coarsened exact matching?
a:
A form of distance matching where the worst acceptable match has zero distance — observations must
be exactly the same on all matching variables.
title: Coarsened Exact Matching and Entropy Balancing

q: Nick is 35, lives in Washington, and has a PhD. Who counts as a match, and what if nobody does?
a:
Only someone who is also 35, also lives in Washington, and also has a PhD. If nobody on the other
side matches, he's dropped from the data.
title: Coarsened Exact Matching and Entropy Balancing

q: Can exact matching be combined with other methods?
a:
Yes — you might allow distance on bill size while requiring an exact match on which state someone
lives in.
title: Coarsened Exact Matching and Entropy Balancing

q: Why is "coarsened" necessary?
a:
Because exact matching on a continuous variable is impossible — nobody else has a bill of exactly
$9,132 — so you bin the variable first and match exactly on the bin.
title: Coarsened Exact Matching and Entropy Balancing

q: Why does coarsened exact matching need no iterative checking?
a:
Because by construction the treated and control groups are the same on average — any difference at
all and the observation is dropped.
title: Coarsened Exact Matching and Entropy Balancing

q: What's the main downside of coarsened exact matching?
a:
It drops a great many observations, giving imprecise and possibly biased results, since anyone
slightly unusual on even one variable gets removed.
title: Coarsened Exact Matching and Entropy Balancing

q: Why does the curse of dimensionality hit CEM extra hard?
a:
Each additional variable must be matched *exactly*, and finding someone identical to you on nine or
ten aspects of life is essentially impossible.
title: Coarsened Exact Matching and Entropy Balancing

q: What should you check after running coarsened exact matching?
a:
How many observations were dropped. If it's a lot, use another method or iterate — bigger bins, or
fewer matching variables.
title: Coarsened Exact Matching and Entropy Balancing

q: When does coarsened exact matching work well?
a:
When the matching variables are broad enough to yield plenty of matches, or when you have millions
of observations.
title: Coarsened Exact Matching and Entropy Balancing

q: What do you specify in entropy balancing?
a:
A set of variables and a set of moments — means, variances, skewness, kurtosis — that you want to be
equal across the treated and control groups.
title: Coarsened Exact Matching and Entropy Balancing

q: Give an example set of entropy balancing requirements.
a:
Mean age equal across groups; variance of age equal; proportion living in Washington equal;
proportion with a PhD equal.
title: Coarsened Exact Matching and Entropy Balancing

q: What does entropy balancing then do?
a:
Finds a set of weights that makes all those requirements true — and if a possible set exists, it does
a good job of finding it.
title: Coarsened Exact Matching and Entropy Balancing

q: Why does entropy balancing need no balance check?
a: Because balance was the input — the conditions you specified have been enforced by construction.
title: Coarsened Exact Matching and Entropy Balancing

q: What can go wrong with entropy balancing?
a: You may request too many conditions for any set of weights to satisfy simultaneously.
title: Coarsened Exact Matching and Entropy Balancing

q: How common is entropy balancing, and what's Nick's view?
a:
It's the least common of the four approaches and relatively new, but he thinks it has a promising
future and may be worth learning.
title: Coarsened Exact Matching and Entropy Balancing

q: Name the three things matching depends on.
a: The conditional independence assumption, common support, and balance.
title: When Matching Goes Wrong

q: State the conditional independence assumption.
a:
That you matched on all the variables needed to close all the back doors, so treatment is
essentially random conditional on your matching variables.
title: When Matching Goes Wrong

q: What are the equivalent phrasings of that assumption?
a:
"Close all the back doors" in the causal diagram context, and "no omitted variable bias" in the
regression context.
title: When Matching Goes Wrong

q: Define common support.
a:
Substantial overlap in the distributions of the matching variables between the treated and control
groups — ranges where there are plenty of people on both sides.
title: When Matching Goes Wrong

q: Give the job training illustration of failed common support.
a:
If a program recruits everyone in one neighbourhood, there's nobody in that neighbourhood who wasn't
treated, and nobody treated outside it — so you can't match on neighbourhood.
title: When Matching Goes Wrong

q: What was Broockman studying, and what was the treatment?
a:
Whether legislators respond to help requests from an apparently Black constituent outside their
district — a fictional Tyrone Washington. The treatment is whether the legislator is Black.
title: When Matching Goes Wrong

q: What's the back door in that study?
a:
The percentage of Black residents in the district — Black legislators are more likely to be elected
there, and district composition may also affect responding for other reasons.
title: When Matching Goes Wrong

q: Describe the common support failure in Broockman's data.
a:
Non-Black legislators are overwhelmingly in districts with few Black residents, while Black
legislators are mostly in districts 50–75% Black — so there aren't enough non-Black legislators to
compare against exactly where most Black legislators are.
title: When Matching Goes Wrong

q: What does common support mean for a propensity score specifically?
a:
You need some treated people with a decent probability of *not* being treated, and some untreated
people with a decent probability of being treated.
title: When Matching Goes Wrong

q: What is "dropping areas of non-common support", and what does it cost?
a:
Removing observations in ranges where there's nobody comparable on the other side. It improves the
remaining matches but can delete the most typical treated observations, leaving an unrepresentative
estimate.
title: When Matching Goes Wrong

q: Why trim very high and very low propensity scores?
a:
Someone with a 90% chance of treatment probably was treated, so there are few controls up there;
someone with a 0.05% chance has few treated observations to compare to.
title: When Matching Goes Wrong

q: What is a balance table?
a:
A comparison of the average of each matching variable, or the propensity score, between the treated
and control groups — ideally before and after matching.
title: When Matching Goes Wrong

q: In Broockman's table, what happened to the Democrat variable?
a:
Before matching, 97.8% of Black legislators were Democrats versus 50.1% of non-Black legislators.
After matching it was exactly 97.8% for both — almost entirely Democratic non-Black legislators were
selected.
title: When Matching Goes Wrong

q: Why isn't a good balance table sufficient?
a:
It compares means, and two distributions can have identical means while looking completely
different in shape.
title: When Matching Goes Wrong

q: What did Broockman's post-matching distributions show?
a:
Percent Black in the district lined up fairly well, but the propensity score distributions did not —
more non-Black legislators at very low and very high scores — which is concerning given the weighting
was done on the propensity score.
title: When Matching Goes Wrong

q: What should you do if balance looks bad?
a:
Go back and redo the matching — add polynomials to the propensity score model, change matching
variables — which is entirely legitimate.
title: When Matching Goes Wrong

q: How do you estimate a treatment effect once matching is done?
a:
Take the weighted average of the outcome in the treated group and in the control group, and
compare them.
title: Treatment Effect Estimation with Matching

q: You matched the control group to look like the treated group. Which average do you get?
a: The average treatment on the treated.
title: Treatment Effect Estimation with Matching

q: And if you matched the treated group to look like the control group?
a: The average treatment on the untreated.
title: Treatment Effect Estimation with Matching

q: How do you get the average treatment effect?
a:
Obtain both the ATT and the ATUT and average them together based on the proportion of people
treated.
title: Treatment Effect Estimation with Matching

q: Why are standard errors harder with matching than with regression?
a: The matching procedure itself injects uncertainty that must be accounted for.
title: Treatment Effect Estimation with Matching

q: What happens if you just regress the outcome on treatment using matching weights?
a:
It fails to account for the uncertainty from the matching process, so your standard errors come out
too small.
title: Treatment Effect Estimation with Matching

q: What's Nick's practical advice on matching standard errors?
a: Let the software you're using calculate them — the computations involve a lot of difficult details.
title: Treatment Effect Estimation with Matching

q: Describe bootstrapping in this context.
a:
Repeatedly draw a new random sample from your data and, each time, redo the entire matching and
estimation process — mimicking the sampling distribution yourself.
title: Treatment Effect Estimation with Matching

q: Why does bootstrapping solve the matching uncertainty problem?
a:
Because you redo the matching on every resample, the uncertainty of the matching process is
incorporated into the resulting distribution.
title: Treatment Effect Estimation with Matching

q: When does bootstrapping not work well?
a:
With procedures that pick observations in or out of the control group, rather than weighted
approaches.
title: Treatment Effect Estimation with Matching

q: Why combine matching with regression?
a:
To get things regression does well — an interaction term, for example, is awkward with a weighted
average but easy in a regression with matching weights applied.
title: Treatment Effect Estimation with Matching

q: What did Broockman actually do?
a:
Applied his matching weights to a regression model containing an interaction term, rather than
taking a plain weighted average.
title: Treatment Effect Estimation with Matching

q: What two models could be misspecified in a matching analysis?
a:
The matching procedure — a model of how treatment gets assigned — and the regression model — a model
of the outcome.
title: Treatment Effect Estimation with Matching

q: What does a doubly robust estimator guarantee?
a:
The right answer as long as at least one of those two models is correctly specified. If both are
wrong, it still fails.
title: Treatment Effect Estimation with Matching

q: Is double robustness a method?
a: No — it's a property that various estimators possess.
title: Treatment Effect Estimation with Matching

q: Give the four steps of inverse probability weighting with regression adjustment.
a:
Estimate the propensity score and get inverse probability weights; use them on just the treated data
to fit an outcome regression; do the same on just the untreated data; then predict everyone's outcome
under both models and compare.
title: Treatment Effect Estimation with Matching

q: Why is that procedure doubly robust?
a:
If the propensity score estimation is misspecified you're saved by a good regression model, and if
the regression model is misspecified you're saved by correctly specified inverse probability weights.
title: Treatment Effect Estimation with Matching

q: What is simulation, in this context?
a:
Generating your own data — typically with a random number generator — applying your methods to it,
and seeing what result you get.
title: Simulation

q: What's the alternative to simulation for checking an estimator's properties, and why is it worse?
a:
Writing a statistical proof. It's very difficult, and unless you think in maths it can be hard to
understand the answer on a gut level.
title: Simulation

q: Give the three reasons to run a simulation.
a:
To find out whether a method works, to find out when and how badly it breaks, and because you get
to pick the truth so you know what the right answer is.
title: Simulation

q: Why is knowing the truth so valuable?
a:
Because with real data you almost never know it. Knowing it means you can tell whether your method
returned the right answer.
title: Simulation

q: Your method returns the wrong answer on simulated data. What are the two possible conclusions?
a: The method is broken, or the data you gave it isn't a good setting in which to apply that method.
title: Simulation

q: Give examples of questions you could ask about omitted variable bias by simulation.
a:
How bad is the bias when you fail to control for something — and is it worse or better if the
omitted variable is strongly or weakly related, negatively or positively related, or more related to
the treatment than the outcome?
title: Simulation

q: What's the recommended procedure for building a simulation study?
a:
Start with data satisfying all the estimator's assumptions and confirm it works there, then break
things one at a time and see what happens.
title: Simulation

q: What are the two kinds of finding you're hunting for?
a:
Assumptions you can break with only a little damage, and assumptions whose violation ruins the
results.
title: Simulation

q: When does Nick personally run a simulation?
a:
Whenever he faces a new method he isn't sure about, or has an idea for an estimator and doesn't
want to do the work of writing a proof — as a first check on whether it seems to work.
title: Simulation

q: Why do these videos avoid simulation code?
a:
The book contains the coding examples in three different languages, and he wasn't going to record
each video three times. Chapter 15 has them.
title: Simulation

q: List the five steps of running a simulation.
a:
Choose the data generating process; generate a sample; apply your estimator; store the estimate;
repeat many times.
title: Running a Simulation

q: Why does step one matter so much?
a:
Because you choose the data generating process yourself, you know the truth — which is what makes
it possible to judge the estimator.
title: Running a Simulation

q: How do you build a causal effect into simulated data?
a:
Use one variable to generate another — draw x from a random distribution, then draw y from a
distribution whose mean is x, so x causes y.
title: Running a Simulation

q: In that setup, what effect should your estimator recover?
a: One — a one-unit increase in x should be associated with a one-unit increase in y.
title: Running a Simulation

q: What was the true value in the coin demonstration?
a: 50% heads, because a fair coin was chosen.
title: Running a Simulation

q: What estimator was tested, and why that one?
a:
The proportion of heads multiplied by two thirds — deliberately chosen because it's wrong, to
demonstrate what failure looks like.
title: Running a Simulation

q: Round one gave 4 heads out of 6. What was the estimate?
title: Running a Simulation

q: Give the four estimates across the four rounds.
a: 8/18, 8/18, 1/3, and 1/3 — from four heads, four heads, three heads, and three heads.
title: Running a Simulation

q: What was the average, and what does it tell you?
a: About 39%, against a truth of 50%. They don't match, so the estimator doesn't work.
title: Running a Simulation

q: Is four iterations enough?
a: No — you'd want a thousand. But you'd still land around 38%, so the conclusion stands.
title: Running a Simulation

q: What do you compare against the truth at the end?
a: The mean of the sampling distribution built from all your simulated estimates.
title: Running a Simulation

q: What does it mean if the mean of the sampling distribution matches the truth?
a: The estimator seems to be working in that context — the data generating process you built.
title: Running a Simulation

q: Name two useful outcomes besides "the estimator failed."
a:
Discovering that something which looked wrong doesn't actually break the answer, and discovering
that a broken assumption makes results only slightly wrong.
title: Running a Simulation

q: What is power analysis asking?
a:
Whether you have enough observations and sample size to actually estimate the thing you want to
estimate.
title: Power Analysis

q: Name the four situations that make statistics' job harder.
a:
Tiny true effects; little variation in the treatment; a lot of noise relative to signal; and high
standards for evidence.
title: Power Analysis

q: Give the parachute and hair-growth contrast.
a:
Whether a parachute saves your life needs one or two observations. Whether a pill grows your hair a
fraction of a centimetre needs an enormous number — tiny effects are hard to spot.
title: Power Analysis

q: Why can't you study whether going to the moon makes you smarter?
a:
Only a couple of people have been, so there's almost no variation in the treatment, and statistics
studies variation.
title: Power Analysis

q: Give the three problems with running an underpowered study.
a:
Very imprecise estimates; a decent chance of missing a real effect; and a decent chance that
anything you do find is a fluke.
title: Power Analysis

q: Why is a significant result from a tiny sample suspicious?
a:
Because with very little data you generally only clear the significance bar when you happen to draw
unusually weird data.
title: Power Analysis

q: List the five quantities in a power analysis.
a:
True effect size, variation in treatment, other variation in the outcome, statistical precision, and
sample size.
title: Power Analysis

q: How does a power analysis work mechanically?
a: You hold four of the five quantities constant and solve for how big the fifth needs to be.
title: Power Analysis

q: What are the two most common questions asked?
a: What's the minimum necessary sample size, and what's the minimum detectable effect size?
title: Power Analysis

q: Explain minimum detectable effect size.
a:
Given a fixed sample size, how big the true effect must be for you to detect it — 30 observations can
only detect very large effects, while a million can detect much smaller ones.
title: Power Analysis

q: When is a power calculator sufficient, and when do you need simulation?
a:
A calculator handles simple settings like a two-group randomized experiment. Simulation is needed for
complex features like hierarchical data structure or complicated treatment assignment.
title: Power Analysis

q: What extra step makes a simulation a power analysis?
a:
Wrapping the whole simulation loop in another iteration that reruns it at different sample sizes or
different true effect sizes.
title: Power Analysis

q: What do you store on each iteration if you're measuring statistical power?
a: Whether or not you rejected the null hypothesis.
title: Power Analysis

q: Your power analysis says you need 100,000 students and you can get 5,000. What should you do?
a: Not run the study. Running poorly powered studies is a bad idea.
title: Power Analysis

q: What problem does this section of the book address?
a: That your causal diagram tells you to control for something you don't have data on.
title: Fixed Effects

q: State the key insight of fixed effects.
a:
If an unmeasured variable is constant within a broader category you *do* observe, controlling for
that category controls for everything inside it.
title: Fixed Effects

q: What kind of data does fixed effects require?
a:
Panel data — the same individuals measured repeatedly over time, whether people, companies, or
countries.
title: Fixed Effects

q: How does controlling for your identity control for your birthplace?
a:
Because your birthplace is fixed within the category of you, so a control for you absorbs everything
unique to you that doesn't change over time.
title: Fixed Effects

q: In the chancellor-visit example, which confounders get bundled into "country"?
a:
Geography, history with Germany, and the culture of the population — all effectively constant within
a country over the timescale of the data.
title: Fixed Effects

q: Which confounder in that example is *not* closed by country fixed effects, and why?
a:
Current politics, because a country's relationship with Germany changes from year to year, so it has
within variation.
title: Fixed Effects

q: Define between variation.
a: Variation between individuals — Austria having different geography than England.
title: Fixed Effects

q: Define within variation.
a:
Variation within an individual over time — Austria's political relationship with Germany changing
year to year.
title: Fixed Effects

q: Can a variable have both?
a: Yes. Politics differs across countries *and* changes within a country over time.
title: Fixed Effects

q: What does controlling for the individual do to these two components?
a: It removes all the between variation, leaving only the within variation.
title: Fixed Effects

q: You: 5 in 2019 and 7 in 2020. Me: 4 and 3. Give the averages and the between variation.
a:
Your average is 6, mine is 3.5. The between variation is that difference — you exercise more on
average.
title: Fixed Effects

q: Give the within variation for both people.
title: Fixed Effects

q: What does fixed effects do with the 6-versus-3.5 difference?
a: Discards it entirely, looking only at how each person varied around their own average.
title: Fixed Effects

q: In the graphical version, what does giving each person their own axes accomplish?
a:
Every point is measured relative to that person's own averages, so you're seeing only within
variation.
title: Fixed Effects

q: What's the punchline about slopes in the exercise-and-colds graph?
a:
The overall relationship slopes upward, but within Nick's own data it slopes vaguely downward —
between and within can point in opposite directions.
title: Fixed Effects

q: Does fixed effects require you to know what the unmeasured confounders are?
a: No. Anything constant within the individual is absorbed whether or not you can name or measure it.
title: Fixed Effects

q: How does a fixed effect differ mathematically from a categorical control variable?
a: It doesn't — they're the same thing. Only the reason for including it differs.
title: Estimating Fixed Effects

q: Why do you add it, if not to control for that variable specifically?
a:
Because that category stands in for a bunch of individual characteristics you can't measure and
want to control for.
title: Estimating Fixed Effects

q: What do the i and t subscripts in a fixed effects model indicate?
a: That the variables vary over both individual and time — that is, you have panel data.
title: Estimating Fixed Effects

q: Why does the intercept get an i subscript?
a:
Because each individual gets their own intercept, which is exactly what a set of binary category
indicators does — being in a category shifts the intercept.
title: Estimating Fixed Effects

q: What's the first way to estimate a fixed effects model, and its downside?
a:
Literally add the categorical control to the regression. The downside is that with thousands or
hundreds of thousands of individuals you'd be estimating that many coefficients, which is very slow.
title: Estimating Fixed Effects

q: Describe the demeaning approach.
a:
Take the average of the outcome and every predictor for each individual, subtract those averages
out, and regress the demeaned outcome on the demeaned predictors.
title: Estimating Fixed Effects

q: How is demeaning the same as the Chapter 4 method of controlling for a variable?
a:
You take what you can predict with the control and subtract the prediction out — and knowing only
which individual it is, the best prediction is that individual's own average.
title: Estimating Fixed Effects

q: You average four hours of exercise a week. What does demeaning leave you with?
a:
The within variation — that this year you exercised one hour more than your average, and another
year one hour less.
title: Estimating Fixed Effects

q: Which estimation method do most fixed-effects packages actually use?
a: Demeaning.
title: Estimating Fixed Effects

q: What standard errors are usually used with fixed effects, and why?
a:
Clustered standard errors, clustered at the level of the fixed effect, because error terms are
likely more related within an individual than between individuals.
title: Estimating Fixed Effects

q: Life expectancy on log GDP per capita with country fixed effects gives 9.769. Interpret it.
a:
Within country, a year in which that country has a one-unit higher log GDP per capita would be
expected to have 9.769 longer life expectancy than that same country in a year with lower log GDP per
capita.
title: Estimating Fixed Effects

q: What is a two-way fixed effects model?
a: One with a fixed effect for individual and a fixed effect for the time period.
title: Estimating Fixed Effects

q: What does the time fixed effect control for?
a:
Shared changes over time — for instance a year in which Germany's economy is booming so everybody
trades more with Germany.
title: Estimating Fixed Effects

q: What's the catch with two-way fixed effects?
a:
They're easy to run but trickier to interpret, since you're looking within individual *and* within
time and those interact — and the models sometimes behave in unexpected ways.
title: Estimating Fixed Effects

q: What does fixed effects do to the between variation?
a: It controls all of it away, leaving only within variation.
title: Random Effects

q: Give the two downsides of that.
a:
You can't study anything that exists only between individuals, and you lose statistical power by
discarding information.
title: Random Effects

q: Why can't fixed effects study the effect of birthplace?
a:
Birthplace has no within variation — it never changes for a person — so the variation you'd need was
exactly what got removed.
title: Random Effects

q: What does random effects do instead?
a:
It constrains the individual effects to follow a distribution rather than removing them, so it doesn't
eliminate all the between variation.
title: Random Effects

q: What's the general trade being made?
a: Adding an assumption buys back statistical power but risks bad results if the assumption is wrong.
title: Random Effects

q: What assumption does plain random effects need, and why is that a problem?
a:
That the individual effect is unrelated to all the control variables and the treatment — which is
usually not the case.
title: Random Effects

q: What does hierarchical linear modeling add?
a:
It uses measurable characteristics — like geography — to predict where an individual sits in the
distribution of individual effects.
title: Random Effects

q: Why does that fix the assumption problem?
a:
Because it's now acceptable for the individual effect to be related to geography — that relationship
is incorporated into the estimation rather than assumed away.
title: Random Effects

q: How common is hierarchical linear modeling?
a:
Very — probably more common than fixed effects in a lot of fields, though economics tends to prefer
fixed effects.
title: Random Effects

q: What extra thing can hierarchical linear modeling do?
a:
Model how the effect of one variable on another varies with individual characteristics, as an
alternative to interaction terms.
title: Random Effects

q: Describe the Mundlak variant of correlated random effects.
a:
Demean each variable as usual and include the demeaned version — but also include the individual's
mean itself in the model.
title: Random Effects

q: What does each of those two versions carry?
a: The mean carries the between variation; the demeaned variable carries the within variation.
title: Random Effects

q: What does including both accomplish?
a: It separates the between and within variation so you can use whatever mix of them you want.
title: Random Effects

q: What does plain random effects give you, versus the advanced versions?
a:
Plain random effects gives a mix of the between and within effect. The advanced versions give you the
between effect and the within effect separately.
title: Random Effects

q: What do random effects' assumptions usually concern?
a:
The relationship between the individual effect — or whatever part of it you haven't modelled with
covariates — and the treatment variable you're interested in.
title: Random Effects

q: What is an event study?
a:
A design where an event occurs at a particular time, and you compare the period after to the
period before to find the effect of what happened.
title: Event Studies

q: What are "canned designs"?
a:
Structures that, if your particular setting applies to them, let you expect to get a causal effect
of interest.
title: Event Studies

q: Give some other names for this design.
a:
Statistical process control, interrupted time series, before-and-after comparison, and sometimes
case control — though that usually means something else.
title: Event Studies

q: How do economists and finance researchers use "event study" differently?
a:
Economists usually mean many events happening to different people at different times, possibly with
a control group. Finance means a single event at a single time, comparing before to after.
title: Event Studies

q: What is the single back door in an event study?
a: Time — it determines whether you're before or after the event, and it also affects the outcome.
title: Event Studies

q: Why can't you close that back door by controlling for time?
a:
Time determines treatment perfectly — 0% treated before, 100% after — so controlling for it removes
all the variation in treatment and leaves nothing to study.
title: Event Studies

q: What do you do instead?
a:
Use the pre-treatment period to predict what the post-treatment period would have looked like, then
compare the actual outcome to that prediction.
title: Event Studies

q: How do you read the comparison?
a: Actual above the prediction means a positive effect; below means negative; equal means zero.
title: Event Studies

q: Describe the first graph and its conclusion.
a:
A flat time series with no trend; after treatment it jumps up and then fades back toward the
expected level — a strong positive effect at the moment of treatment, fading over time.
title: Event Studies

q: Describe the second graph and its conclusion.
a:
An upward-trending series; after treatment the outcome drops, contrary to the trend — a negative
treatment effect.
title: Event Studies

q: Describe approach 1 and when it's acceptable.
a:
Simply compare after outcomes to before outcomes. It's defensible when the pre-treatment data shows
no trend at all and you're confident nothing would have changed.
title: Event Studies

q: Describe approach 2.
a:
Fit a time trend in the pre-treatment data and assume it would have continued; the deviation from
that trend is your treatment effect.
title: Event Studies

q: When does approach 2 work best?
a: When there's good reason to believe the pre-existing trend would have continued.
title: Event Studies

q: Describe approach 3, and why it isn't circular.
a:
Use post-treatment data on *other* observable variables — not the outcome — that the outcome tracked
closely before treatment, and see whether the outcome deviates from them.
title: Event Studies

q: Give the beer and wine example.
a:
Before a beer ban, beer and wine prices move in lockstep. After it, wine continues as normal and
beer deviates — and that deviation from the expected tracking is the treatment effect.
title: Event Studies

q: What's the most common application of approach 3?
a:
Financial markets — a stock that tracks the market as a whole and then deviates when the event
occurs.
title: Event Studies

q: Describe the interrupted time series method.
a:
Fit a time trend in the pre-treatment period, fit another in the post-treatment period, and look at
where they meet at the cutoff to see whether there's a jump.
title: Interrupted Time Series

q: When is it a good method to use?
a:
When you think there's a consistent trend before, another consistent trend after, and you're
interested in the difference between them.
title: Interrupted Time Series

q: What later design is it mathematically similar to?
a: Regression discontinuity.
title: Interrupted Time Series

q: What three terms go into the regression implementation?
a: Time (a continuous measure), a binary indicator for being after treatment, and their interaction.
title: Interrupted Time Series

q: What's the crucial adjustment to the time variable?
a: Subtract the cutoff date so that time equals zero at the moment the event goes into effect.
title: Interrupted Time Series

q: Why does centring time make interpretation straightforward?
a:
Because the intercept is the prediction at time zero, and time zero is now the event — so the
intercepts are the predictions at the cutoff.
title: Interrupted Time Series

q: Give the intercept and slope in the pre-treatment period.
a: Intercept β₀ and slope β₁ — with `after` equal to zero, the β₂ and β₃ terms fall away.
title: Interrupted Time Series

q: Give the intercept and slope in the post-treatment period.
a: Intercept β₀ + β₂ and slope β₁ + β₃.
title: Interrupted Time Series

q: Which coefficient is the treatment effect, and why?
a:
β₂ — it's the difference between the two intercepts, which is the jump in the prediction at the
event date.
title: Interrupted Time Series

q: What did the ambulance quality improvement study find?
a:
No jump at the cutoff, so no immediate effect — but a steeper post-treatment slope, suggesting the
programme improved things slowly over time.
title: Interrupted Time Series

q: How do you allow for non-straight lines?
a: Add polynomial terms, interacting each of them with the after indicator as well.
title: Interrupted Time Series

q: Why is functional form choice so consequential here?
a:
Because a curve makes its strangest predictions near the edges, and the edge — the event date — is
exactly where you're measuring the jump.
title: Interrupted Time Series

q: What's the standard error problem, and what specifically goes wrong?
a:
Autocorrelation — one period's observation is correlated with the next's. Ignoring it makes you
over-reject the null, so you'll claim effects that don't exist.
title: Interrupted Time Series

q: What does an event study actually require?
a:
A prediction of the counterfactual and a comparison against it — not necessarily a straight-line
regression.
title: Event Studies in Finance

q: Why are effects immediate in finance?
a: Because stock prices respond to the *expectation* of change rather than to literal physical change.
title: Event Studies in Finance

q: Give the contrast used to illustrate that.
a:
An education programme's effect on college graduation wouldn't show up for about ten years; Apple's
stock price moves ten seconds after an announcement.
title: Event Studies in Finance

q: What's the first benefit of immediate effects?
a:
Predictions over a very short horizon are far more reliable, so the counterfactual is more credible
and there's no time for other effects to sink in.
title: Event Studies in Finance

q: What's the second consequence of immediacy, and what problem does it create?
a:
The effect happens all at once and then fades. Fitting a time-series trend line to the post-period
makes no sense when you need an effect that appears once and disappears.
title: Event Studies in Finance

q: List the four steps of the finance approach.
a:
Pick an estimation period and an observation period; make predictions; compare to get abnormal
returns; analyse those abnormal returns.
title: Event Studies in Finance

q: What is an abnormal return?
a:
The difference between the actual return and the return you predicted from the prior pattern — a
predicted 3% against an actual 6% gives a 3-point abnormal return.
title: Event Studies in Finance

q: What do you do in step four?
a:
Analyse the abnormal returns — possibly one per day or per treated group — including calculating
standard errors.
title: Event Studies in Finance

q: Describe the mean-adjusted returns approach.
a:
Take the average from the estimation period and use it as the prediction for the observation
period, assuming no trend at all.
title: Event Studies in Finance

q: Why is that assumption more reasonable than it sounds for stocks?
a:
Stock *levels* may climb steadily, but daily *returns* are fairly flat — roughly the same percentage
each day.
title: Event Studies in Finance

q: Describe the market-adjusted returns approach and the back door it closes.
a:
Use the market's return as the prediction for the stock. It prevents confusing a rise caused by the
event with a rise caused by a good day for the entire market.
title: Event Studies in Finance

q: Apple rose 6% and the market rose 2%. What's the market-adjusted abnormal return?
title: Event Studies in Finance

q: What is market-adjusted returns equivalent to?
a:
Using a control group — the market as a whole stands in for what Apple would have done absent
treatment.
title: Event Studies in Finance

q: How does the risk-adjusted approach differ?
a:
It doesn't assume the stock moves one-for-one with the market; it estimates the relationship
between them and applies that relationship to the post-treatment period.
title: Event Studies in Finance

q: Apple typically moves twice as much as the market, the market rose 2%, and Apple rose 6%. What's the abnormal return?
a: You'd have predicted 4%, so the abnormal return is 2%.
title: Event Studies in Finance

q: What are the Fama-French factors used for here?
a:
Predicting a stock from the returns of particular portfolios — such as ones buying small stocks and
selling big ones — not just the overall market return.
title: Event Studies in Finance

q: Give the non-finance equivalent of each of the three methods.
a:
Mean-adjusted returns is statistical process control; market-adjusted returns is using a control
group; risk-adjusted returns is predicting an outcome from factors it tracks and assuming the
relationship persists.
title: Event Studies in Finance

q: What are the minimum conditions for a difference-in-differences design?
a:
A treatment that goes into effect at a particular time, and that treatment affecting some people
and not others.
title: Difference-in-Differences

q: What problem is it solving that event studies couldn't?
a:
A before/after comparison where you don't trust any of the event-study methods for closing the time
back door.
title: Difference-in-Differences

q: Why can't you close the time back door by controlling for time?
a:
Time determines treatment completely — nobody treated before, everybody after — so controlling for
time removes all treatment variation.
title: Difference-in-Differences

q: What does adding a control group do to the causal diagram?
a:
It makes it worse — adding a second back door through which group you're in, on top of the time
back door.
title: Difference-in-Differences

q: What two things does the treated group's before/after change contain?
a: The effect of treatment, and whatever would have changed over time anyway.
title: Difference-in-Differences

q: Why doesn't fixed effects logic solve this on its own?
a:
Within variation controls for things constant over time but doesn't control for anything that
*changes* over time — which is exactly the concern.
title: Difference-in-Differences

q: What's the placebo-test intuition for the control group?
a:
If the time back door didn't matter, the untreated group shouldn't change at all. If they don't,
you can compare the treated group before and after directly.
title: Difference-in-Differences

q: The control school gains 5 points and the treated school gains 8. What's the effect?
a: 3 points — five of the eight would have happened anyway, judging by the control school.
title: Difference-in-Differences

q: State the design in one sentence.
a:
Take the treated group's before-to-after difference, subtract the control group's before-to-after
difference, and what remains is the treatment effect.
title: Difference-in-Differences

q: What was miasma theory?
a:
The prevailing belief that bad smells transmitted disease — hence the beaked masks packed with
herbs.
title: Difference-in-Differences

q: What was the treatment in John Snow's study?
a:
The Lambeth water company moving its water source from the middle of the Thames to a point
upstream, before the city's sewage entered the river.
title: Difference-in-Differences

q: Who were the treated and control groups?
a:
Treated: areas supplied by Lambeth, whose source changed. Control: areas supplied by other pumps,
whose sources didn't.
title: Difference-in-Differences

q: Give the four death rates per 10,000.
a: Non-Lambeth: 134.9 before, 146.6 after. Lambeth: 130.1 before, 84.9 after.
title: Difference-in-Differences

q: Compute the difference-in-differences.
a:
Lambeth fell by 45.2, non-Lambeth rose by 11.7, so the estimate is about 56.9 fewer deaths per
10,000.
title: Difference-in-Differences

q: Why wasn't Lambeth's drop alone convincing?
a: Cholera epidemics had always burned out on their own, so the rate might have fallen regardless.
title: Difference-in-Differences

q: What ruled that out?
a: The non-Lambeth death rate didn't fall — it rose. So the epidemic wasn't burning out on its own.
title: Difference-in-Differences

q: State parallel trends informally.
a:
The change over time observed in the control group is the change the treated group would have
experienced had it not been treated.
title: Parallel Trends

q: State it more precisely.
a:
The gap between the treated and control groups would have stayed the same from before to after, had
treatment not occurred.
title: Parallel Trends

q: Is it a problem if the two groups differ at baseline?
a: No. Baseline differences are fine — it's the *amount* by which they differ that must stay constant.
title: Parallel Trends

q: Why can't you check parallel trends?
a:
It's a claim about what would have happened to the treated group without treatment, which you never
observe.
title: Parallel Trends

q: Give failure mode 1 with its example.
a:
The groups were naturally trending together or apart. Using China as a control for a US policy fails
because China had already been growing much faster for decades.
title: Parallel Trends

q: Give failure mode 2 with its example.
a:
Something else changed for the control group at the same time. If the comparison school started free
school lunches when the treated school started its teacher reform, their change reflects both.
title: Parallel Trends

q: What's the first way to make parallel trends plausible?
a:
Know your context well enough to be confident nothing else changed for the control group at the time
treatment began.
title: Parallel Trends

q: What's the second, and why isn't it strictly required?
a:
Pick comparable groups. It isn't required because baseline differences are permitted — but it makes
the assumption far more believable.
title: Parallel Trends

q: How could you pick comparable groups more rigorously?
a: Use matching procedures to select untreated groups that look very similar to the treated group.
title: Parallel Trends

q: What's the third way, and what does it actually look at?
a:
Check prior trends — look at multiple pre-treatment periods to see whether the two groups were
already moving in parallel.
title: Parallel Trends

q: Give the prior trends example.
a:
A teacher reform starting in 2012 with data from 2005 to 2011; if both schools' maths scores improve
at roughly the same rate over that window, they'd plausibly have continued to.
title: Parallel Trends

q: Does checking prior trends prove parallel trends?
a: No. Something like a free lunch programme could still begin exactly when treatment does.
title: Parallel Trends

q: Describe parallel prior trends on a graph.
a:
Both groups increase, at similar rates, ending the last pre-treatment period at roughly the same gap
they had at the first period observed.
title: Parallel Trends

q: Describe converging prior trends and what they imply.
a:
The groups start far apart and get steadily closer before treatment, meaning the control group is
trending up faster. Its time effect is larger than the treated group's, so subtracting it won't work.
title: Parallel Trends

q: Describe the four means approach.
a:
Compute the treated group's average before and after and subtract; do the same for the control
group; then subtract the control group's difference from the treated group's.
title: Estimating Difference-in-Differences

q: What is forced choice, and how does it differ from opt-in and opt-out?
a:
You still have to opt in, but you're required to answer the question — yes for yes, no for no —
rather than being able to ignore it. It sits between opt-in and opt-out.
title: Estimating Difference-in-Differences

q: What did Kessler and Roth study, and what was the treatment?
a:
Whether forced choice raises organ donation rates. California adopted forced choice in July 2011,
compared against other comparable states.
title: Estimating Difference-in-Differences

q: Give the four means.
a: Control states 44.5 before and 45.9 after; California 27.1 before and 26.3 after.
title: Estimating Difference-in-Differences

q: Compute the difference-in-differences and interpret it.
a:
(26.3 − 27.1) − (45.9 − 44.5) = −0.8 − 1.4 = −2.2 percentage points — the policy appears to have
reduced donation rates by 2.2 points.
title: Estimating Difference-in-Differences

q: Why isn't California's 0.8-point drop the answer on its own?
a:
Because the control states rose by 1.4, so California would likely have risen too. The shortfall is
2.2, not 0.8.
title: Estimating Difference-in-Differences

q: What are the two limitations of the four means approach?
a: It's hard to obtain standard errors, and it doesn't extend beyond two groups and two time periods.
title: Estimating Difference-in-Differences

q: What three terms go in the regression version?
a:
An indicator for being in the treated group, an indicator for being after treatment, and their
interaction.
title: Estimating Difference-in-Differences

q: What does each coefficient represent?
a:
β₀ is the untreated group before; β₀ + β₁ the treated group before, so β₁ is the baseline
difference; β₀ + β₂ the untreated group after, so β₂ is the time effect; and all four terms give the
treated group after.
title: Estimating Difference-in-Differences

q: Which coefficient is the difference-in-differences effect, and why?
a:
β₃ — because going from before to after, the untreated group rises by β₂ while the treated group
rises by β₂ + β₃, so subtracting the time effect leaves β₃.
title: Estimating Difference-in-Differences

q: What three terms make up the two-way fixed effects model?
a:
A fixed effect for group, a fixed effect for time, and a variable equal to one if you're in a
treated group during a post-treatment period.
title: Estimating Difference-in-Differences

q: What does each of the two fixed effects accomplish?
a:
The group fixed effect keeps you working within within-variation; the time fixed effect subtracts
out the shared time effects estimated from the control group.
title: Estimating Difference-in-Differences

q: How should standard errors usually be handled here?
a: Clustered by group, since observations within a group are likely more similar than across groups.
title: Estimating Difference-in-Differences

q: What is the crucial limitation shared by every method in this episode?
a: They only work if all treatments went into effect at the same time.
title: Estimating Difference-in-Differences

q: What is staggered treatment, and what can you do about it?
a:
When different groups get treated at different times — California in July 2011 and Wyoming in July
2012. You can still analyse it, but not with any of these regression methods.
title: Estimating Difference-in-Differences

q: What are the three ways to support parallel trends?
a: Think through the context, check prior trends, and run placebo tests.
title: Supporting Parallel Trends

q: What does checking prior trends involve?
a:
Looking at pre-treatment data to see whether the treated and control groups were trending in the
same direction at the same rate before treatment.
title: Supporting Parallel Trends

q: What do you need in order to check prior trends at all?
a: Multiple periods of pre-treatment data — otherwise there's no trend to measure.
title: Supporting Parallel Trends

q: Describe the regression that tests prior trends.
a:
Regress the outcome on group fixed effects and a time trend allowed to differ by group, with time
entering as a linear variable rather than as fixed effects.
title: Supporting Parallel Trends

q: What are the two groups' time trends in that regression?
a: The control group's is β₁; the treated group's is β₁ + β₂.
title: Supporting Parallel Trends

q: What does β₂ tell you?
a:
The difference between the two slopes. If it differs from zero, the groups are trending differently
— evidence against parallel trends.
title: Supporting Parallel Trends

q: What if the trend isn't linear?
a: Estimate it in whatever way fits — add a polynomial and interact that polynomial with group as well.
title: Supporting Parallel Trends

q: Why should you be cautious about a non-significant β₂?
a:
With only a few pre-treatment periods you may lack the statistical power to reject zero even when
the trends genuinely differ.
title: Supporting Parallel Trends

q: What's the general lesson about tests where you want a non-significant result?
a:
You have to ask whether you had the power to detect a real difference — and inspect the graph
visually as well.
title: Supporting Parallel Trends

q: Explain why prior trends and parallel trends aren't the same thing.
a:
Prior trends can hold perfectly while parallel trends fails — if the control school starts a free
lunch programme exactly when the treated school starts its reform, the groups tracked identically
beforehand but would have diverged regardless of treatment.
title: Supporting Parallel Trends

q: What is a placebo test?
a:
A test that should yield an effect of zero if your assumptions are correct; a non-zero result
indicates something is missing from your model.
title: Supporting Parallel Trends

q: Walk through the placebo test for a 2012 reform.
a:
Drop all data from 2012 onward, keep 2008–2011, pretend treatment occurred in 2009, and estimate
difference-in-differences. You should find zero.
title: Supporting Parallel Trends

q: What does a non-zero placebo result suggest?
a:
That the trends differ in a period when no treatment occurred, so they likely wouldn't have matched
during the real treatment period either.
title: Supporting Parallel Trends

q: What's the second way to run this check?
a:
A dynamic difference-in-differences model, which estimates effects across many periods at once —
you check that the pre-treatment effects are zero.
title: Supporting Parallel Trends

q: What do these checks actually establish?
a:
Nothing conclusive. Failing them makes parallel trends unlikely; passing them makes the assumption
more plausible and gives you an argument, but never proof.
title: Supporting Parallel Trends

q: What do economists call this model, and why is that confusing?
a:
An event study — which is what Chapter 17 called an entirely different method. Here it's dynamic
difference-in-differences.
title: Dynamic Difference-in-Differences

q: What does dynamic difference-in-differences do differently?
a:
Rather than comparing all pre-treatment periods to all post-treatment periods, it estimates a
separate effect for each period.
title: Dynamic Difference-in-Differences

q: With a 2012 reform, describe the comparisons being made.
a:
2011 against 2013, 2011 against 2014, 2011 against 2015, and so on — plus pre-treatment comparisons
like 2011 against 2010 and 2009.
title: Dynamic Difference-in-Differences

q: Why not force a single treatment effect?
a:
Many treatments take time to do anything, or do a lot at first and fade. There's no reason to assume
the same effect applies in every period.
title: Dynamic Difference-in-Differences

q: How do you estimate it?
a:
Start with the two-way fixed effects model and interact the treated indicator with a set of time
period fixed effects.
title: Dynamic Difference-in-Differences

q: Why must one period be omitted?
a:
You need a reference group — a baseline before-period that every other coefficient, including the
other pre-treatment ones, is compared against.
title: Dynamic Difference-in-Differences

q: What do β₋₁ and β₁ represent?
a: The effect in the last period before treatment, and the effect in the first period after treatment.
title: Dynamic Difference-in-Differences

q: Why does the reference period appear with no confidence interval?
a: It's the baseline everything is compared to, not an estimate.
title: Dynamic Difference-in-Differences

q: What did the dynamic organ donation estimates show?
a:
A large significant negative effect of about −0.02 in Q3 2011, roughly the same in Q4, and a similar
but much less precise estimate in Q1 2012 — an immediate drop that then stayed put.
title: Dynamic Difference-in-Differences

q: Give an example of an effect that grows over time.
a:
