When gas prices jump, people buy more fuel-efficient cars — but it takes time to buy a car, so the
effect starts small and grows.
title: Dynamic Difference-in-Differences

q: What would happen to that effect under a static model?
a:
You'd probably lose it entirely — averaging a growing effect with the near-zero early periods can
make it disappear.
title: Dynamic Difference-in-Differences

q: Why are pre-treatment coefficients estimated at all?
a: They're placebo tests — they should be zero, since no treatment had occurred.
title: Dynamic Difference-in-Differences

q: What does a non-zero pre-treatment coefficient mean?
a:
The gap between groups was already changing when no treatment was in effect, so the groups were
trending at different rates — prior trends violated, and parallel trends in doubt.
title: Dynamic Difference-in-Differences

q: What did the organ donation data show in Q1 2011, and how concerned should you be?
a:
A statistically significant effect in a pre-treatment period. The deviation isn't large, so it isn't
alarming — though it isn't a good look. A large one would be seriously concerning.
title: Dynamic Difference-in-Differences

q: List what the dynamic model lets you see.
a:
Whether the effect is immediate, whether it stays constant, whether it grows, whether it fades — and
whether the pre-treatment placebo effects are zero.
title: Dynamic Difference-in-Differences

q: What limitation does it still share with the other models?
a: It assumes all treatment happened at the same time for everybody.
title: Dynamic Difference-in-Differences

q: What is a staggered treatment or rollout design?
a:
A difference-in-differences setup where multiple groups become treated at different times — one
school district in 2012, another in 2013, another never.
title: Staggered Treatment Difference-in-Differences

q: What did researchers long assume about estimating it?
a:
That you could just use two-way fixed effects, since group fixed effects handle baseline differences
and time fixed effects handle shared changes.
title: Staggered Treatment Difference-in-Differences

q: What are the two ways a unit can show no change in treatment status?
a: It was never treated and still isn't, or it was already treated and continues to be.
title: Staggered Treatment Difference-in-Differences

q: Why does that matter?
a:
Because two-way fixed effects treats both as controls — so already-treated units are used as a
control group when they aren't one.
title: Staggered Treatment Difference-in-Differences

q: When would that be survivable?
a:
If the already-treated group took its jump and then trends stayed the same, parallel trends could
still hold.
title: Staggered Treatment Difference-in-Differences

q: What makes it not survivable?
a: Treatment effects that evolve over time — then already-treated units are still deviating from trend.
title: Staggered Treatment Difference-in-Differences

q: Walk through the 2012 and 2013 schools example.
a:
If the programme takes time to work, the 2013 cohort is compared against a control set including the
already-treated 2012 school, whose effect is only now kicking in. That school deviates from trend,
violating parallel trends.
title: Staggered Treatment Difference-in-Differences

q: Is this a problem with difference-in-differences itself?
a:
No. Difference-in-differences doesn't mind multiple groups treated at different times. It's the
two-way fixed effects estimator forcing already-treated groups to act as controls.
title: Staggered Treatment Difference-in-Differences

q: What two things do all the new estimators allow?
a:
Effects that differ by cohort — when you started being treated — and effects that differ by time
period.
title: Staggered Treatment Difference-in-Differences

q: What do you do after estimating all those effects?
a:
Aggregate them into whatever single effect you want — an overall average, or an average by years
since treatment.
title: Staggered Treatment Difference-in-Differences

q: How does Callaway and Sant'Anna choose control groups?
a:
With matching in each period, using only units not actually treated at that time, and optionally
including or excluding units that will eventually be treated.
title: Staggered Treatment Difference-in-Differences

q: What weighting does it often use?
a: Inverse probability weighting, as in the matching chapter.
title: Staggered Treatment Difference-in-Differences

q: What estimator underlies the Wooldridge approach, and what does it do?
a:
The Mundlak estimator — group fixed effects for within variation while also controlling for the
between variation, so both are in the model at once.
title: Staggered Treatment Difference-in-Differences

q: What's the key difference between Wooldridge–Mundlak and dynamic difference-in-differences?
a:
You interact not just with the time period but with **which** treated cohort you're in — whether you
started being treated in 2012 or 2013.
title: Staggered Treatment Difference-in-Differences

q: What is the bottom-line instruction?
a:
You must use one of these methods for staggered treatment. Two-way fixed effects can bias you
terribly.
title: Staggered Treatment Difference-in-Differences

q: Is there a settled standard estimator?
a: No — it's a fertile area of research, with no consensus at the time of writing.
title: Staggered Treatment Difference-in-Differences

q: How does this class of methods differ from everything before it?
a:
Earlier methods find back doors and control them away. Instrumental variables isolates the front
door path instead.
title: Instrumental Variables

q: Give the statue analogy.
a:
Carving away marble until the statue emerges is closing back doors; pouring metal into a mould so
only the desired shape can result is finding a front door.
title: Instrumental Variables

q: What is an instrumental variable, in one sentence?
a:
A source of randomization existing in the world that you isolate statistically — a randomized
experiment you didn't get to run.
title: Instrumental Variables

q: In the drug trial, what's random and what isn't?
a:
Random assignment to be told to take the drug is random; whether people actually took it is not,
because they chose whether to comply.
title: Instrumental Variables

q: Define exogenous.
a: As good as random in the system you're studying — with no back doors from it to your outcome.
title: Instrumental Variables

q: What makes random assignment an instrument here?
a:
It affects whether you actually took the drug, so it supplies a random component to a treatment
that isn't otherwise randomly assigned.
title: Instrumental Variables

q: Contrast the mechanics of a control variable and an instrument.
a:
With a control variable you predict and subtract that prediction out. With an instrument you predict
and keep only the prediction, discarding everything else.
title: Instrumental Variables

q: In the two-value graphs, what do you compute in step one?
a: The average x when z = 1 and the average x when z = 0.
title: Instrumental Variables

q: What happens to all the other variation?
a: It's discarded — everything collapses to the two predicted points, since z takes only two values.
title: Instrumental Variables

q: Why does the line between the two collapsed points have a causal interpretation?
a:
Because whether you fall in the z = 1 or z = 0 group is randomly assigned, so the change in x is
random and the resulting change in y is randomly determined by treatment.
title: Instrumental Variables

q: State the relevance assumption and why it matters.
a:
The instrument must actually predict the treatment. If it doesn't, isolating the part of treatment
it explains leaves no variation to study — no matter how genuinely random the instrument is.
title: Instrumental Variables

q: State the validity assumption.
a:
The instrument really is a source of exogenous variation — randomly assigned, or randomly assigned
conditional on controls that close any back doors between it and the outcome.
title: Instrumental Variables

q: Does instrumental variables eliminate the back door problem?
a:
No. It moves the requirement from a difficult variable, the treatment, to one that's hopefully
easier, the instrument.
title: Instrumental Variables

q: State the validity assumption precisely.
a: The instrument is unrelated to the outcome except through the treatment.
title: Instrumental Variable Validity

q: Why is that stronger than "no back doors"?
a:
It also forbids the instrument from causing anything else that reaches the outcome — no other front
doors, not just no back doors.
title: Instrumental Variable Validity

q: What happens if the instrument causes something else?
a:
When you isolate the part of treatment determined by the instrument, you also pick up part of that
other thing.
title: Instrumental Variable Validity

q: What does the diagram you're claiming look like?
a:
Instrument → treatment → outcome, with an uncontrollable back door affecting the treatment, and no
path from the instrument to the outcome except via treatment.
title: Instrumental Variable Validity

q: What dominates the questions at an IV seminar?
a: Whether the instrument is genuinely valid — whether you can believe it's exogenous.
title: Instrumental Variable Validity

q: Why is validity especially hard in social science?
a:
Because everything is related to everything else, so it's implausible that one variable sits apart,
unrelated to everything important.
title: Instrumental Variable Validity

q: How have standards changed over time?
a:
A couple of decades ago people would assert an instrument was random without much scrutiny. Now
people are far more sceptical, and several once-accepted instruments are doubted.
title: Instrumental Variable Validity

q: What was rainfall used as an instrument for?
a:
Agricultural productivity — since it seems random whether it rains more or less in a given year,
conditional on region and season.
title: Instrumental Variable Validity

q: Give the first problem with rainfall.
a:
It's correlated between neighbouring farms, so your neighbour's productivity moves with yours and
reaches your outcome by a route other than your own productivity.
title: Instrumental Variable Validity

q: Give the Sarsons finding and why it's damaging.
a:
Rainfall's effect on agricultural productivity is roughly the same in regions with irrigation dams
as without — which shouldn't happen if the mechanism is water access, suggesting something else is
driving the relationship.
title: Instrumental Variable Validity

q: What kind of question is instrument validity?
a:
An inherently theoretical one — can you defend the claim that your causal diagram has no back door
and no alternative front door from instrument to outcome?
title: Instrumental Variable Validity

q: What is a canonical instrumental variables design?
a:
An instrument/treatment pairing used across many contexts and heavily scrutinised, which people
still generally believe is exogenous.
title: Instrumental Variable Validity

q: Name five canonical designs.
title: Instrumental Variable Validity

q: What are the two families canonical designs fall into?
a:
Those with actual explicit randomization, and those involving things that seem entirely outside the
system being modelled.
title: Instrumental Variable Validity

q: Give the wind direction example.
a:
If a city's pollution comes from the east, the wind blowing one way brings it in and the other way
carries it out — pretty random, and it's hard to see why wind would affect anything else.
title: Instrumental Variable Validity

q: What happens if validity doesn't hold?
a: Instrumental variables won't work, and there's not a lot you can do about it.
title: Instrumental Variable Validity

q: Describe the two stages of two-stage least squares.
a:
First, use the instrument and controls to predict the treatment. Second, regress the outcome on that
predicted treatment along with the controls.
title: Estimating Instrumental Variables

q: Why does that eliminate omitted variable bias?
a:
Because you're using only the part of treatment predicted by exogenous variation — the random part —
and random variation can't be related to the error term.
title: Estimating Instrumental Variables

q: What extra step does two-stage least squares require?
a:
Adjusting the standard errors to account for the fact that the predicted treatment was estimated
rather than observed.
title: Estimating Instrumental Variables

q: Is two-stage least squares the same as instrumental variables?
a: No — it's one estimator for an instrumental variables model, among several.
title: Estimating Instrumental Variables

q: What assumption does generalized method of moments build on?
a: That the instrument is unrelated to the error term in the second-stage equation.
title: Estimating Instrumental Variables

q: What are moment conditions here?
a:
Statements that the instrument should be uncorrelated with the residuals, with coefficients chosen so
that holds.
title: Estimating Instrumental Variables

q: Give the two advantages of generalized method of moments over two-stage least squares.
a:
It performs better under heteroskedasticity, giving smaller and more accurate standard errors, and it
handles multiple instruments better.
title: Estimating Instrumental Variables

q: Who is a never taker, and how do they affect the estimate?
a:
Someone who wouldn't take the treatment regardless of assignment. They drop out of the estimate
entirely, however large their treatment effect would have been.
title: Estimating Instrumental Variables

q: Whose effect does the estimate reflect?
a:
People who don't take the treatment when not assigned and do take it when assigned — weighted more
heavily the more strongly they respond.
title: Estimating Instrumental Variables

q: Give the exercise example of response-weighting.
a:
If finger-wagging makes one person exercise one more hour a week and another five more hours, you get
far more of the five-hour person's effect in your estimate.
title: Estimating Instrumental Variables

q: What is this kind of estimate called?
a: A local average treatment effect.
title: Estimating Instrumental Variables

q: Why might two different instruments for the same treatment give different answers?
a:
They move different people. Finger-wagging and a photo of a fit person appeal to different
populations, so each isolates a different part of the treatment effect.
title: Estimating Instrumental Variables

q: In the rural China study, what were the instrument, treatment, and outcome?
a:
Instrument: whether your friend was randomly assigned to the high-pressure "default buy" setting.
Treatment: whether your friend bought insurance. Outcome: whether you bought insurance.
title: Estimating Instrumental Variables

q: Give the first stage result and what it establishes.
a:
Random assignment to the "assume you buy" setting raised your friend's probability of buying by 11.8
percentage points — establishing relevance, with a large effect, so there's variation to work with.
title: Estimating Instrumental Variables

q: Give the second stage result.
a:
Your friend buying insurance makes you 79.1 percentage points more likely to buy — a very strong
network effect.
title: Estimating Instrumental Variables

q: Name the three assumptions mentioned, including the one not covered in detail.
a:
Relevance, validity, and monotonicity — the instrument must affect everyone's probability of
treatment in the same direction.
title: Testing Instrumental Variables Assumptions

q: How do you check relevance?
a: Look at how closely related the instrument and treatment are.
title: Testing Instrumental Variables Assumptions

q: Why are weak instruments so damaging?
a:
You use only the part of treatment predicted by the instrument, so weak prediction leaves very little
variation to work with, giving estimates that can vary by thousands of percentage points across samples.
title: Testing Instrumental Variables Assumptions

q: What is small sample bias in instrumental variables?
a:
Instrumental variables is a biased estimator in finite samples, because the instrument's relationship
with the error term won't be exactly zero in a given sample even if it is in the population.
title: Testing Instrumental Variables Assumptions

q: When does small sample bias become a serious problem?
a:
With a really small sample or a really weak instrument — the weaker the instrument, the more of your
estimate is bias rather than effect.
title: Testing Instrumental Variables Assumptions

q: What does the first stage F statistic test?
a:
The joint significance of your instruments in the first stage — whether all their coefficients are
zero.
title: Testing Instrumental Variables Assumptions

q: Why is "F above 10" the wrong way to think about it?
a:
Because a bigger F means smaller instrumental variables bias *relative to* the ordinary least squares
bias — the bias doesn't disappear until F is enormous, on the order of 105.
title: Testing Instrumental Variables Assumptions

q: What should you consult instead?
a:
The Stock and Yogo tables, which show how large F must be to keep small sample bias small relative to
the OLS bias.
title: Testing Instrumental Variables Assumptions

q: How does using an F cutoff create publication bias?
a:
With a genuinely weak instrument, whichever researcher happens to draw a sample where it looks strong
proceeds, while others don't — so the study that gets published is the fluke.
title: Testing Instrumental Variables Assumptions

q: What's the recommended alternative?
a:
Proceed with the study but use Anderson–Rubin confidence intervals, which account for a possibly weak
instrument.
title: Testing Instrumental Variables Assumptions

q: What's unusual about those intervals?
a:
They're wider, and not necessarily symmetric — the interval may be larger on one side of the estimate
than the other.
title: Testing Instrumental Variables Assumptions

q: Why is validity hard to test empirically?
a:
It's inherently theoretical — an assumption about the relationship between your instrument and things
you can't necessarily observe. Empirical tests for it rather miss the point.
title: Testing Instrumental Variables Assumptions

q: Describe the Durbin–Wu–Hausman test.
a:
Estimate twice — once with instrumental variables and once with plain ordinary least squares — and
compare. Differing coefficients suggest instrumental variables is correcting something; similar ones
suggest you may as well use OLS.
title: Testing Instrumental Variables Assumptions

q: What is Nick's objection to it?
a:
It amounts to treating a failure to reject a null hypothesis as proof that you don't have an
endogeneity problem.
title: Testing Instrumental Variables Assumptions

q: What is that test actually testing?
a:
The presence of endogeneity in your OLS — the exogeneity of your treatment — not the validity of your
instrument.
title: Testing Instrumental Variables Assumptions

q: Describe the over-identification test.
a:
With more than one instrument, assume one is valid and use it as ground truth: compare results using
only that instrument against results using both. Differences suggest the second may be invalid.
title: Testing Instrumental Variables Assumptions

q: Give Nick's two objections to it.
a:
It again treats failure to reject a null as evidence of validity; and differing results may simply
reflect that the two instruments isolate different local average treatment effects, so you might discard
a perfectly valid instrument.
title: Testing Instrumental Variables Assumptions

q: What structure does regression discontinuity require?
a: A smooth change in some variable followed by a sudden change at a cutoff.
title: Regression Discontinuity

q: Give the San Diego to Tijuana figures.
a:
Median income around $85,000 in San Diego, about $50,000 in San Ysidro just north of the border, and
about $25,000 in Tijuana just across it.
title: Regression Discontinuity

q: What makes that a discontinuity rather than just a decline?
a:
It takes a few miles to fall from 85 to 50, but only a few feet to fall from 50 to 25 — a sudden
change at a specific point.
title: Regression Discontinuity

q: Define the running variable.
a:
The continuous variable that changes smoothly and determines whether you fall above or below the
cutoff.
title: Regression Discontinuity

q: Define the bandwidth and explain why it matters.
a:
A narrow range around the cutoff. It matters because comparing everyone on each side confounds the
treatment with the running variable itself — but people just either side of the cutoff have nearly the
same value of the running variable.
title: Regression Discontinuity

q: Name three settings where regression discontinuity applies.
a:
Geographic borders (including policing jurisdictions), close elections, and policies with a
qualifying threshold.
title: Regression Discontinuity

q: How do close elections form a discontinuity?
a:
Vote share moves smoothly, but a tiny change from 50.01% to 49.9% dramatically changes who won — so
you can compare the effect of having one politician rather than another.
title: Regression Discontinuity

q: Why are people earning $12,001 and $11,999 comparable?
a:
They're essentially the same on every characteristic you can imagine, and which side you land on is
almost random — someone arriving fifteen minutes early once could tip it.
title: Regression Discontinuity

q: Is this a back door or front door design?
a:
A front door design, like instrumental variables — it isolates variation that's as good as random
rather than measuring and controlling confounders.
title: Regression Discontinuity

q: Why can't you control for the running variable directly?
a: It's the only thing determining treatment, so controlling for it leaves no treatment variation.
title: Regression Discontinuity

q: What are you actually controlling for instead?
a: All parts of the running variable except the portion just around the cutoff.
title: Regression Discontinuity

q: How highly is regression discontinuity regarded?
a:
Probably the most trusted design after randomized experiments — some government policies accept
evidence as high quality only from randomized experiments or regression discontinuity designs.
title: Regression Discontinuity

q: What reservation does Nick raise?
a: He suspects there may be statistical power problems with regression discontinuity.
title: Regression Discontinuity

q: Why are regression discontinuity graphs so convincing?
a:
Because there's no real effect from the running variable before the cutoff, none after, but a clear
change exactly at the point treatment changes.
title: Regression Discontinuity

q: What limits where you can use it?
a: It only applies where treatment is assigned based on a cutoff.
title: Regression Discontinuity

q: What three terms go into the basic regression discontinuity regression?
a: The running variable minus the cutoff, an indicator for being treated, and their interaction.
title: Estimating Regression Discontinuity

q: Why subtract the cutoff from the running variable?
a:
So the running variable equals zero at the cutoff, which makes the intercepts the predicted outcomes
at the cutoff — the point where the two lines meet.
title: Estimating Regression Discontinuity

q: Cutoff is $12,000 and you earn $12,005. What's your centred running variable?
title: Estimating Regression Discontinuity

q: Give the intercept and slope on each side.
a: Untreated side: intercept β₀, slope β₁. Treated side: intercept β₀ + β₂, slope β₁ + β₃.
title: Estimating Regression Discontinuity

q: Which coefficient is the regression discontinuity effect?
a: β₂ — the jump between the two intercepts at the cutoff.
title: Estimating Regression Discontinuity

q: What other method is this mathematically identical to?
a: The interrupted time series approach from the event studies chapter, estimated with linear regression.
title: Estimating Regression Discontinuity

q: What distinguishes the two designs, given identical mathematics?
a:
The running variable. Event studies use time, and the assumption that people just before and just
after an event are comparable is much weaker than the assumption for a non-time cutoff.
title: Estimating Regression Discontinuity

q: Why is "randomly on one side" more plausible than "randomly before or after"?
a:
You can't really randomly be before or after a date, whereas landing just above or below a threshold
genuinely can be near-random.
title: Estimating Regression Discontinuity

q: What's the problem with the basic regression as first specified?
a: It uses the entire range of the data, when the design's logic only holds near the cutoff.
title: Estimating Regression Discontinuity

q: State the bandwidth trade-off.
a:
Narrow bandwidths give more credible identification but drop so much data that estimates get very
noisy; wide bandwidths give precision but bring back less comparable observations and possible bias.
title: Estimating Regression Discontinuity

q: Give two ways to choose a bandwidth.
a:
Optimal bandwidth selection procedures, or trying several bandwidths and checking whether the estimate
is stable as they widen.
title: Estimating Regression Discontinuity

q: What does stability across bandwidths tell you?
a: That widening isn't biasing you much, so you might as well use the extra data for precision.
title: Estimating Regression Discontinuity

q: Why does functional form matter so much in regression discontinuity?
a:
Because you're extrapolating each line to the cutoff, and a poorly fitting shape puts the line in the
wrong place exactly where you're measuring the jump.
title: Estimating Regression Discontinuity

q: How do you add a quadratic to the model?
a: Include the squared centred running variable and interact that with the treated indicator as well.
title: Estimating Regression Discontinuity

q: How far up the polynomial orders should you go?
a: Usually no further than a quadratic.
title: Estimating Regression Discontinuity

q: What is fuzzy regression discontinuity?
a:
When the running variable only partially determines treatment — for instance, qualifying below
$12,000 but also having to apply, so 0% are treated above and only 50% below.
title: Estimating Regression Discontinuity

q: How do you estimate a fuzzy design?
a:
Use being on one side of the cutoff as an instrument for actually being treated, and apply
instrumental variables.
title: Estimating Regression Discontinuity

q: What two problems do these adjustments address?
a:
How wide to make the bandwidth, and what functional form to use — both of which matter because the
estimate at the cutoff must be as accurate as possible.
title: Adjustments to Regression Discontinuity

q: What's the basic idea behind weighting in regression discontinuity?
a:
Let observations closest to the cutoff count most in estimating the line, with influence declining as
you move away.
title: Adjustments to Regression Discontinuity

q: What is a kernel function?
a:
A function centred around zero, typically tallest at zero, that descends as you move away until
reaching a weight of zero.
title: Adjustments to Regression Discontinuity

q: Name two kernels and how they differ.
a: The triangular kernel declines in a straight line; the Epanechnikov kernel is more curved.
title: Adjustments to Regression Discontinuity

q: What does weighting buy you over a hard bandwidth?
a:
It lets you include more observations for precision while reducing their influence, splitting the
difference between precision and bias.
title: Adjustments to Regression Discontinuity

q: Describe local regression.
a:
Move along the running variable in small windows, fitting a separate regression inside each one and
producing predictions just for the data in that range.
title: Adjustments to Regression Discontinuity

q: How does local regression handle curvature?
a:
It lets the slope change continuously — flat where the data is flat, steeper where it's steeper — so
the fitted line can take any shape.
title: Adjustments to Regression Discontinuity

q: Why does local regression incorporate the bandwidth automatically?
a:
Because estimating the regression at any point uses only observations within that small window, which
is itself a bandwidth or weighting.
title: Adjustments to Regression Discontinuity

q: What's the cost of local regression?
a:
Less precision — more flexibility almost always costs precision, and regression discontinuity is
already precision-poor.
title: Adjustments to Regression Discontinuity

q: Why do you need fewer polynomials with local regression?
a:
Because within a small window, almost any curve looks linear — so a linear fit inside each window is
usually enough.
title: Adjustments to Regression Discontinuity

q: Give the image for that.
a: The horizon looks like an almost straight line, because it's a very zoomed-in view of a round Earth.
title: Adjustments to Regression Discontinuity

q: What's the first assumption regression discontinuity requires?
a: That people just either side of the cutoff are comparable except that some got the treatment.
title: Checking Regression Discontinuity Assumptions

q: Give the college degree example of that failing.
a:
20% of those just below the cutoff have college degrees against 40% of those just above — a large
difference between supposedly identical groups.
title: Checking Regression Discontinuity Assumptions

q: What two things could that difference mean?
a: Something else changes at the cutoff — such as another policy — or you simply drew an unlucky sample.
title: Checking Regression Discontinuity Assumptions

q: How do you check for it?
a:
Run regression discontinuity again using your control variables as the dependent variable, and hope
you find no jump.
title: Checking Regression Discontinuity Assumptions

q: Which variables should you use for that check?
a: Anything you'd consider a back door control — anything the treatment shouldn't affect.
title: Checking Regression Discontinuity Assumptions

q: What was the main finding in the government transfers study?
a:
Support for the government jumps sharply right at the cutoff, with smoothly changing and lower
support just above it.
title: Checking Regression Discontinuity Assumptions

q: What two covariates were used in the placebo check, and what did they show?
a:
Mean household age and mean household education. Age rises gently with income with no jump at the
cutoff; education is barely related to income and also shows no jump.
title: Checking Regression Discontinuity Assumptions

q: What do you do if a covariate does jump and you think something real is happening at the cutoff?
a:
Be seriously concerned about the research design — it doesn't work if the two sides aren't
comparable.
title: Checking Regression Discontinuity Assumptions

q: What do you do if you think it's just a bad random draw?
a:
Add a control variable. There's nothing wrong with adding controls to a regression discontinuity
specification, and it can even out sampling differences.
title: Checking Regression Discontinuity Assumptions

q: State the general principle behind these placebo tests.
a:
Find parts of your data where the regression discontinuity effect should not exist, and check whether
it does.
title: Checking Regression Discontinuity Assumptions

q: What's the second assumption?
a:
That people aren't manipulating their running variable in order to land on a particular side of the
cutoff.
title: Checking Regression Discontinuity Assumptions

q: Does the running variable have to be exogenous?
a:
No — it can be related to plenty of back door variables. It just mustn't be something people
deliberately change.
title: Checking Regression Discontinuity Assumptions

q: Give the manipulation example.
a:
Knowing you get a check below $12,000 and being on track for $12,100, you work a little less on
purpose to qualify — so you chose your side rather than being assigned to it.
title: Checking Regression Discontinuity Assumptions

q: How do you check for manipulation?
a:
Bin the running variable, count observations per bin, and see whether the distribution is smooth
across the cutoff.
title: Checking Regression Discontinuity Assumptions

q: What exactly do you hope to see?
a:
That you can't find where the cutoff is — a distribution looking just as it would if nobody cared
about the running variable.
title: Checking Regression Discontinuity Assumptions

q: What can you do if you find manipulation?
a: Not a great deal — it's much harder to deal with, though some tricks exist.
title: Checking Regression Discontinuity Assumptions

q: What is granularity, and why is it a problem?
a:
A running variable measured only in coarse chunks — such as knowing only that income fell between
$12,000 and $13,000 — which forces comparisons between people who are too far apart to be comparable.
title: Checking Regression Discontinuity Assumptions

q: What is heaping, and when does it cause trouble?
a:
Some people report precise values while others round to whole thousands, piling observations at round
numbers. It's a problem if the people who round differ systematically from those who don't, since that
introduces back doors.
title: Checking Regression Discontinuity Assumptions
